28: vehaya acharei chen eshpoch et ruchi al kol basar venivve'u bneichem uvnotechem ziknechem chalomot yachalomun bachureichem cheziyonot yiru: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.' The universal Spirit-outpouring prophesied in Joel 2:28-32 is the most significant single prophecy cited in the NT's account of Pentecost. The key phrase is kol basar (all flesh): unlike the selective, temporary Spirit-anointing of specific judges, prophets, and kings in the OT, the new covenant Spirit will be given to all — removing the mediatorial hierarchy that required prophets and priests to relay YHWH's word to the people. This is Jeremiah 31:34 ('no longer will each person teach his neighbor, for they will all know me') from the pneumatological angle.
1: tiqĕʿu shofar bĕTsiyon wĕhariʿu bĕhar qodshi: “Blow the trumpet in Zion; sound the alarm on my holy mountain!” The shofar (ram’s horn) was used for multiple purposes: warning of attack (Num 10:9; Neh 4:20), assembly for worship (Ps 81:3; Lev 25:9), and the eschatological trumpet (Isa 27:13; Matt 24:31; 1 Cor 15:52). Here it is the alarm of imminent invasion. Harʿiʿu (sound the alarm) — ruaʿ (to shout, blow an alarm; cf. the trumpet blasts at Jericho, Josh 6) — is the battle alarm distinct from the cultic trumpet blast. The combination of shofar + Zion + holy mountain positions this as both military and eschatological: the Day of the LORD is simultaneously a military invasion and a theophanic event.
2: yom choshekh waʾaphelah yom ʿanan waʾaraphel kĕshachar parash ʿal heharim: “A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness; like the dawn spreading over the mountains, there comes a great and mighty people.” The four darkness terms — choshekh (darkness), ʾaphelah (gloom, thick darkness), ʾanan (cloud), ʾaraphel (dense darkness, the cloud of theophany at Sinai: Exod 20:21; Deut 4:11) — accumulate to describe maximum obscurity. Kĕshachar parash ʿal heharim (like dawn spread over the mountains) — the simile is deliberately paradoxical: dawn is associated with light, but here it describes the locust army’s advance; the locusts are so dense they appear like the dark leading edge of sunrise spreading across the horizon.
3: lĕpanayw ʾakĕlah ʾesh wĕʾacharayw tĕlahet lahav: “Before them a fire devours, and behind them a flame burns; before them the land is like the garden of Eden, behind them a desolate wilderness.” The garden of Eden (gan ʿEden) appears here as the image of maximal agricultural fertility — the land at its pristine best. The locusts’ passage transforms Eden into a midbar shĕmamah (desolate wilderness). This garden-to-wilderness reversal is the theological anti-trajectory: creation moving backwards from ordered abundance toward primordial chaos. The same imagery will be reversed in the restoration of vv. 21-27.
4: kĕmarʾeh susim marʾehu ukĕpharashim ken yarutsun: “Their appearance is like that of horses; and like war horses, so they charge.” The locust-horse comparison is one of the most noted images in ancient natural history: the locust’s head does resemble a horse’s head (hence the German Heuschrecke = “hay horse” and the Italian cavalletta = little horse). The German and Italian terms suggest this comparison was widely observed across cultures. Revelation 9:7 applies the same imagery to the eschatological locust swarm from the abyss: “the locusts were like horses prepared for battle.”
5: kĕqol markavot ʿal rashĕ heharim yĕraqĕdun: “Like the rumbling of chariots they leap over the mountaintops.” The acoustic description of the locust army — chariot rumble, fire crackling through stubble, a mighty army set in battle array — captures what witnesses of large locust swarms describe: the sound of millions of locust wings produces a distinctive roar. Kĕqol lahav ʾesh ʾokhelah qash (like the crackling of a flame of fire consuming the stubble) — the locust’s consumption of vegetation produces a sound like fire running through dry grass. Kĕʿam ʿatsum ʿarukh milchamah (like a strong people set in array for battle) — the military metaphor is complete.
6: mippanayw yachilu ʿammim kol panim qibĕtsu phaʾrur: “Before them peoples writhe in anguish; every face turns pale.” Yachilu (writhe, be in anguish, from chul/chil = to writhe as in labor pains) — the pain of imminent judgment. Qibĕtsu phaʾrur (every face turns pale/draws in color) — phaʾrur is debated: it may mean “pot-color” (i.e., blackened, soot-colored; from a pot) or “glow” or “pallor.” The LXX reads chytra (pot). The MKT follows the majority reading of paleness/pallor as the most contextually appropriate response to terror.
7: kĕgibbourim yarutsun kĕʾanshei milchamah yaʿalu chomah: “Like warriors they run; like soldiers they scale the wall.” The military metaphor reaches its apex: the locusts scale walls like disciplined troops in a siege. Gibbourim (mighty men, warriors; cf. the gibbore chayil, the mighty men of valor) and ʾanshei milchamah (soldiers, men of war) are the two standard military designations. Wĕlo yĕʿavĕtun ʾorĕchotam (each keeps to his own course; they do not swerve from their paths) — the locusts’ disciplined advance is a marvel of natural history that observers consistently note: they move in one direction, parting around obstacles but not deviating from their course.
8: wĕʾish ʾachiv lo yidchaqun: “They do not push each other; each advances in his own track.” The discipline of the locust swarm continues: no internal conflict, no crowding, no jostling. Uvĕʿad hashelach yippolun lo yivtsaʿu (they burst through the weapons without being stopped) — shelach is a javelin or missile weapon; the locusts pass through defensive weaponry without injury. The untouchability of the army adds to the theological point: this is no ordinary enemy that can be repelled by military means.
9: baʿir yĕshoqĕqun baChomah yĕritsun babbatim yaʿalu bevʿad hachallonut yavoʾu kagannav: “They rush through the city; they run along the walls; they climb up into the houses; they enter through the windows like a thief.” The progressive penetration of the locust army from the city perimeter (rushing through the streets) to the walls to the houses to the windows mirrors the inexorable advance of an invading force that no fortification can stop. The comparison to a thief (kagannav) entering through windows anticipates the NT’s application of the thief-image to the coming of the Son of Man (Matt 24:43-44; 1 Thess 5:2) — the unexpected, unstoppable intrusion of judgment.
10: lĕpanayw ragĕzah ʾerets raʿashu shamayim shemesh wĕyareach qadaru wĕkochavim ʾasĕfu nogĕham: “Before them the earth quakes, the heavens tremble; the sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.” The locust army has now triggered cosmic disruption: earth quakes (ragaz), heavens tremble (raʿash), sun and moon darken. The cosmic signs of vv. 10-11 overlap with those of vv. 30-31 (the Spirit-outpouring oracle); Joel consistently links the locust plague with the eschatological Day of the LORD. The darkening of the sun and moon (from the density of the locust swarm) is simultaneously natural phenomenon and apocalyptic sign.
11: wĕYHWH natan qolo liphnei cheylo ki rav mĕʾod machanehu: “The LORD utters his voice before his army, for his forces are exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful.” YHWH is now explicitly identified as the commander of the locust army: cheylo (his army, his force). Natan qolo (uttered his voice) = YHWH’s thunder; the divine voice accompanies the theophany as in Ps 29 (“the voice of the LORD”) and Exod 19:16. Ki gadol yom YHWH wĕnora mĕʾod (for the day of the LORD is great and very terrible) — nora (awesome, fear-inducing, from yaraʾ) combines terror and reverence; the LXX renders it epiphanēs (glorious, manifest) — a significant divergence that the NT picks up.
12: wĕgam ʿattah nĕʾum YHWH shuvu ʿaday bĕkol lĕvavĕkhem uvĕtsom uvĕvchi uvĕmisped: “‘Yet even now,’ declares the LORD, ‘return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning.’” Wĕgam ʿattah (yet even now) — the adversative introduces the divine grace window: despite the severity of the judgment just described, there is still time to return. Shuvu ʿaday (return to me) — shuv (to return, repent) is the prophetic word for repentance, expressing a turning of direction back toward YHWH. The three accompanying acts — fasting (tsom), weeping (bĕkhi), mourning (mispĕd) — are the external expressions of genuine internal contrition.
13: wĕqirĕʿu lĕvavĕkhem wĕʾal bigdĕkhem wĕshuvu ʾel YHWH ʾeloheikhem: “Rend your hearts and not your garments.” The command qirĕʿu lĕvavĕkhem (rend your hearts) takes the standard mourning gesture — tearing (qaraʿ) one’s garments (Gen 37:29; 2 Sam 1:11; Job 1:20) — and internalizes it: the heart must be torn, not merely the cloth. The external mourning gesture is not forbidden but is meaningless without the internal reality. The divine attributes formula that follows — channun wĕrachum ʾerekh ʾappayim wĕrav chesed wĕnicham ʿal haraʿah (gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting of disaster) — quotes Exodus 34:6-7, the “great confession” that is the OT’s most concentrated statement of YHWH’s character, repeated across the Hebrew Bible (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; 145:8; Jonah 4:2).
14: mi yodea yashuv wĕnicham wĕhishʾir ʾacharayw bĕrakah: “Who knows? He may turn and relent and leave behind a blessing.” Mi yodea (who knows?) is the formula of contingent hope — not expressing ignorance but opening a space of genuine possibility. The same formula appears at Jonah 3:9 (the Ninevites’ hope that God might relent) and 2 Samuel 12:22 (David’s fasting for the sick child). The possibility of YHWH leaving behind a blessing is expressed concretely: minchah wĕnesek lĕYHWH ʾeloheikhem (a grain offering and a drink offering for the LORD your God) — the very offerings whose cessation was mourned in 1:9 could be restored.
15: tiqĕʿu shofar bĕTsiyon qaddĕshu tsom qirʾu ʿatsarah: “Blow the trumpet in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.” The shofar blast of v. 1 (alarm of invasion) is now redirected: the same instrument that announced judgment now summons the assembly for repentance. The trumpet has a double function in Joel — it both warns and calls to prayer. This verse echoes 1:14 almost exactly, but now the call comes after the description of the Divine Warrior’s army, making the urgency more acute.
16: ʾispu ʿam qaddĕshu qahal qibĕtsu zĕqenim ʾispu ʿolalim wĕyonĕqe shadayim: “Gather the people; consecrate the congregation; assemble the elders; gather the children and the nursing infants.” The assembly is comprehensively inclusive: elders (zĕqenim), children (ʿolalim), nursing infants (yonĕqe shadayim) — even those too young to fast or pray are brought to the assembly. The inclusion of the nursing infant is the most extreme extension: the entire community, including those incapable of understanding, is gathered before YHWH. Yetse chatan mechedro wĕchallah mechuppateha (let the bridegroom leave his bedroom and the bride her canopy) — even the newly married, normally exempt from military service and social obligations (Deut 24:5), must attend the communal fast.
17: bein haʾulam wĕlammizbeach yivku hakkohenim mĕshartei YHWH: “Between the vestibule (ʾulam) and the altar let the priests weep and say, ‘Spare your people, O LORD.’” The location — between the ʾulam (vestibule/porch of the temple) and the altar of burnt offering — is the specific priestly space of intercession in the temple court. Ezekiel 8:16 describes the abomination of men worshiping the sun in this same space, facing east; Joel restores the space to its proper use: priestly intercession facing YHWH. The prayer chus ʿal ʿammekha (spare your people) = have compassion; chus (to have pity, spare) is the visceral word for the compassion that withholds punishment.
18: wayĕqanneʾ YHWH lĕʾartso wayachmol ʿal ʿammo: “Then the LORD became zealous for his land and had compassion on his people.” The divine response: qannaʾ (jealous, zealous — the same root as the divine attribute “I the LORD your God am a jealous God,” Exod 20:5; Deut 4:24) and racham (had compassion, from rechem = womb; the deep maternal compassion). YHWH’s response to Israel’s repentance is characterized by the same intensity of emotion — jealousy/zeal for his land and people — that characterized his judgment.
19: wayaʿan YHWH wayoʾmer lĕʿammo hinnĕni sholeach lĕkhem ʾet hadagan wĕhattirosh wĕhayitshar: “The LORD answered and said to his people: ‘Behold, I am sending you grain, wine, and oil.’” YHWH’s answer to the priestly prayer (v. 17) is the restoration of the three Deuteronomic covenant blessings — dagan (grain), tirosh (new wine), and yitshar (oil) — exactly the three whose loss was mourned in 1:10. The reversal is complete and precise. Wĕloʾ ʾetten ʾetkhem ʿod cherpah baggoyim (I will no more make you a disgrace among the nations) — the shame of covenant curse (Deut 28:37) is removed.
20: wĕʾet hatsĕphoni ʾarchiq meʿaleikhem: “I will drive the northern invader far from you.” HaTsĕphoni (the northern one) — the locust army is now described geographically as coming from the north. Major locust migrations historically moved northeast-to-southwest across the Fertile Crescent; however, the “north” (tsaphon) also carried mythological significance in Canaanite religion as the home of Baal and the divine assembly (cf. Ps 48:2; Isa 14:13). The identification of the northern invader’s van into the eastern sea (Dead Sea) and rear into the western sea (Mediterranean) = complete expulsion from the land from east to west.
21: ʾal tiraʾi ʾadamah gili wĕsimchi ki higdil YHWH laʿasot: “Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for the LORD has done great things.” The personified land (ʾadamah) that mourned in 1:10 is now commanded to rejoice. Higdil YHWH laʿasot (the LORD has done great things) — the language of the Psalms of praise (Ps 126:2-3: “The LORD has done great things for us; we are glad”). The reversal of mourning into joy is expressed through the same vocabulary in both directions.
22: ʾal tiraʾu behemot sadeh ki dasheʾu neʾot midbar ki ʿets nasaʾ piryo: “Fear not, you wild animals, for the pastures of the wilderness are green again.” The wild animals that groaned (1:18) and cried out to YHWH (1:20) are also addressed in the restoration. The wilderness pastures are green (dasheʾu, from desheʾ = fresh vegetation), the trees bear fruit, the fig tree and vine give full yield — exact reversals of the locust plague’s devastation.
23: uvnei Tsiyon gilu wĕsimchu bĕYHWH ʾeloheikhem ki natan lĕkhem ʾet hamoreh litsĕdaqah: “Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in the LORD your God, for he has given the early rain as a sign of his faithfulness.” Hamoreh litsĕdaqah is the verse’s philological crux. Moreh has two possible meanings: (1) early rain (yoreh, the autumn rain) — which is the most natural reading in an agricultural context and fits the immediate verse (followed by “early rain and the spring rain”); (2) teacher — the Qumran community (Moreh haTsĕdaqah, the Teacher of Righteousness) understood this verse as a prophecy of their eschatological leader. Litsĕdaqah = either “in/according to righteousness” (modifying YHWH’s character) or “for righteousness” (the purpose of the teacher). The MKT follows the agricultural reading: “the early rain as a sign of his faithfulness” — tsĕdaqah here rendered as “faithfulness” in the covenantal sense (YHWH’s righteous keeping of his promises).
24: umaleʾu hagĕranot bar wĕheshiqu hayayqavim tirosh wĕyitshar: “The threshing floors shall be full of grain; the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.” The threshing floor (goren) and wine/oil vats (yeqev) are the loci of agricultural processing — the endpoints of the harvest cycle. Their overflowing fulfills the reversal of 1:17 (empty storehouses and ruined granaries). The language echoes the covenant blessing of Deuteronomy 28:8 (“The LORD will command the blessing on you in your barns and in all that you undertake”) and Proverbs 3:10 (“your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will be bursting with wine”).
25: wĕshillamti lĕkhem ʾet hashanim ʾasher ʾakhal haʾarbeh hayelek wĕhechasil wĕhagazam: “I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten.” YHWH promises to restore (shalam, the causative of shalom = to make complete, to repay) — the same root as shalom (wholeness/peace) and the concept of making restitution. The restoration covers “the years” — not just the current year’s harvest but the accumulated agricultural deficit. The four locust terms recur in reverse order (arbeh, yelek, chasil, gazam vs. 1:4’s gazam, arbeh, yelek, chasil), possibly signaling a deliberate reversal of the destruction sequence.
26: waʾakhaltem ʾakol wĕsavoaʿ uhillaltem ʾet shem YHWH ʾeloheikhem: “You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the LORD your God.” The covenant cycle of abundance → satisfaction → praise is the Deuteronomic ideal (Deut 8:10: “you shall eat and be full, and you shall bless the LORD your God”). Wĕloʾ yevoshu ʿammi lĕʿolam (my people shall never again be ashamed) — the shame (bosheth) of the covenant curse (Deut 28:37; cf. Joel 1:11) is permanently removed. This phrase is repeated in v. 27, creating a double assurance of the end of shame.
27: wiyĕdaʿtem ki bĕqerev Yisraʾel ʾani wĕʾani YHWH ʾeloheikhem wĕʾein ʿod: “You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am the LORD your God and there is none else.” Wĕyĕdaʿtem (you shall know) — the recognition formula that the prophets use for the eschatological moment when YHWH’s identity becomes undeniable through his saving acts (Ezek’s “and they shall know that I am the LORD”). Wĕʾein ʿod (and there is none else) is the strict monotheistic formula of Deutero-Isaiah (Isa 45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22; 46:9), here applied to YHWH’s presence among Israel. The agricultural restoration produces theological knowledge.
29: wĕgam ʿal haʿavadim wĕʿal hashaphachot bayyamim hahem ʾeshpoch ʾet ruchi: “Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.” ʿAvadim wĕshaphachot (male and female servants/slaves) — the extension of the Spirit gift to the lowest social stratum of ancient Israel makes the universality of v. 28 (“all flesh”) concrete: it includes even those with no social standing. The LXX and Peshitta preserve “my servants” and “my handmaidens” (which Peter quotes in Acts 2:18 as “my male servants and my female servants”), emphasizing that these slaves become servants of YHWH, not merely servants of their human masters.
30: wĕnatatti mophĕtim bashamayim uvʾarets dam wĕʾesh wĕtimrot ʿashan: “And I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke.” Mophĕtim (wonders, portents) — the same word used for the Egyptian plagues in Exodus (Exod 7:3; 11:9-10). The cosmic signs — blood (dam), fire (ʾesh), columns of smoke (timrot ʿashan, literally “palm-tree columns of smoke,” the vertical shape of a smoke column) — are associated with both theophany and warfare. The cosmic signs accompany the pouring of the Spirit, situating the Spirit-outpouring within the eschatological Day of the LORD.
31: hashemesh yehaphekh lĕchoshekh wĕhayareach lĕdam lifnei bo yom YHWH haggadol wĕhannora: “The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.” Hashemesh yehaphekh lĕchoshekh (the sun turned to darkness) — haphak (to overturn, turn upside down; used for the overthrow of Sodom, Gen 19:25) describes a violent inversion. Hayayreach lĕdam (the moon to blood) — the blood-red moon describes a lunar eclipse or smoke-obscured moon during warfare or disaster. The phrase yom YHWH haggadol wĕhannora (the great and awesome Day of the LORD) = the intensified form of the Day formula, reaching its eschatological maximum. Peter quotes this verse (Acts 2:16-21) as inaugurated but not yet consummated at Pentecost.
32: wĕhayah kol ʾasher yiqraʾ bĕshem YHWH yimmalet: “And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved.” The universality of kol ʾasher yiqraʾ (everyone who calls) matches the universality of “all flesh” in v. 28. Bishem YHWH (on the name of the LORD) — the Tetragrammaton; Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:13 and applies it to Jesus (Kyrios), making the most direct christological substitution of the divine Name in the NT: “because the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him” (Rom 10:12). Yimmalet (shall be saved/escape) from malat (to escape, be delivered) — the same root used for Lot’s escape from Sodom (Gen 19:22); salvation conceived as escape from destruction on the Day of YHWH.
